# Act 4: Consulting Entry {.section .focus-section}

---

## 26. Ideal Entry

<p class="slide-kicker">Transformation becomes actionable when executive legitimacy meets operational reality.</p>

<div class="entry-mandate">
<div class="entry-side executive">
<span>Top-down legitimacy</span>
<strong>Committed Executive Sponsor</strong>
<div class="entry-list">
<i>Names the strategic pain</i>
<i>Protects the experiment boundary</i>
<i>Provides access to teams and signals</i>
<i>Owns the resulting decision</i>
</div>
</div>

<div class="entry-bridge">
<b>Mandate</b>
<span>One painful question</span>
<span>One measurable experiment</span>
<span>One explicit decision</span>
</div>

<div class="entry-side reality">
<span>Bottom-up reality</span>
<strong>Embedded Delivery Context</strong>
<div class="entry-list">
<i>Reveals actual dependencies</i>
<i>Tests assumptions in daily work</i>
<i>Surfaces cultural friction safely</i>
<i>Produces observable evidence</i>
</div>
</div>
</div>

<div class="entry-foundation">
<div>
<span>Trust foundation</span>
<strong>OSS + sovereign AI</strong>
<small>Inspectable, locally operable, and grounded in client-specific context.</small>
</div>
<div>
<span>Operating principle</span>
<strong>Trust before transformation</strong>
<small>Effect before method. Observation before judgment. Compatibility before perfection.</small>
</div>
</div>

<div class="entry-punchline">
Top-down legitimacy. Bottom-up reality. Evidence-driven expansion.
</div>

---

## 27. Transformation Pressure Point

<p class="slide-kicker">Do not sell a transformation framework. Operationalize a strategic pain the organization already feels.</p>

<div class="pressure-grid">
<div class="pressure-card">
<span>Speed</span>
<strong>Time to market keeps growing</strong>
<p>Dependencies, approvals, and unclear ownership delay valuable change.</p>
<em>Signal: lead time, deployment frequency, decision latency</em>
</div>
<div class="pressure-card">
<span>Product</span>
<strong>Output rises while impact stalls</strong>
<p>Roadmaps advance, but customer behavior and business outcomes do not.</p>
<em>Signal: adoption, conversion, retention, KPI movement</em>
</div>
<div class="pressure-card">
<span>Architecture</span>
<strong>The monolith blocks learning</strong>
<p>Every experiment becomes a migration discussion or a high-risk release.</p>
<em>Signal: change failure rate, coupling, recovery time</em>
</div>
<div class="pressure-card">
<span>Cost</span>
<strong>Technology spend loses causality</strong>
<p>Cloud, platform, and delivery costs grow without a visible strategic effect.</p>
<em>Signal: cost per outcome, capacity allocation, waste</em>
</div>
<div class="pressure-card">
<span>AI</span>
<strong>AI adoption fragments context</strong>
<p>Teams accelerate locally while language, culture, and decisions drift further apart.</p>
<em>Signal: duplicated work, policy violations, inconsistent decisions</em>
</div>
<div class="pressure-card">
<span>Organization</span>
<strong>Transformation fatigue sets in</strong>
<p>New roles and rituals redistribute responsibility without transferring control.</p>
<em>Signal: escalations, meeting loops, employee sentiment</em>
</div>
</div>

<div class="pressure-punchline">
The entry point is credible when leadership can name the pain, teams recognize it, and reality can answer it.
</div>

---

## 28. Operational Entry

<p class="slide-kicker">Operational excellence can earn strategic access, but only when observed friction is translated into visible impact.</p>

<div class="operational-entry">
<div class="operational-step">
<span>01</span>
<strong>Deliver</strong>
<p>Join as an embedded DevOps or cross-functional engineer and solve real delivery problems.</p>
</div>
<div class="operational-arrow">→</div>
<div class="operational-step">
<span>02</span>
<strong>Earn trust</strong>
<p>Become useful to the team before interpreting its organization.</p>
</div>
<div class="operational-arrow">→</div>
<div class="operational-step focus">
<span>03</span>
<strong>Surface friction</strong>
<p>Use concrete situations to expose repeated decisions, hidden assumptions, and missing control.</p>
</div>
<div class="operational-arrow">→</div>
<div class="operational-step learning">
<span>04</span>
<strong>Demonstrate effect</strong>
<p>Compare decision time, iterations, risk clarity, and delivery impact before and after.</p>
</div>
<div class="operational-arrow">→</div>
<div class="operational-step">
<span>05</span>
<strong>Expand the mandate</strong>
<p>Translate repeated patterns into a jointly owned strategic intervention.</p>
</div>
</div>

<div class="operational-principles">
<i>Trust before transformation</i>
<i>Effect before method</i>
<i>Observation before judgment</i>
<i>Temporary side channels must become unnecessary</i>
</div>

<div class="operational-punchline">
Observable operational impact earns the right to address structural causes.
</div>

---

## 29. High-Risk Feature

<p class="slide-kicker">The first engagement is deliberately small, production-relevant, and designed to earn the next decision.</p>

<div class="mandate-frame">
<div class="mandate-question">
<span>Decision question</span>
<strong>Can we improve checkout completion without increasing operational risk?</strong>
</div>

<div class="mandate-definition">
<div>
<span>Hypothesis</span>
<strong>A clearer payment recovery flow reduces abandonment.</strong>
</div>
<div>
<span>Intervention</span>
<strong>Replace one legacy surface through Composer.</strong>
</div>
<div>
<span>Evidence</span>
<strong>Conversion, errors, latency, and user feedback.</strong>
</div>
<div>
<span>Decision rule</span>
<strong>Scale, revise, stop, or restore the control.</strong>
</div>
</div>

<div class="mandate-constraints">
<i><b>Scope</b>One product surface</i>
<i><b>Timebox</b>Days to first exposure</i>
<i><b>Safety</b>Reversible test group</i>
<i><b>Ownership</b>One executive decision owner</i>
<i><b>Learning</b>Canonical decision record</i>
</div>
</div>

<div class="mandate-punchline">
The deliverable is not software. It is a decision the organization can defend with evidence.
</div>
